meta.title: Civilizational AI Governance Stack — 2026-2050+ Synthesis for Financial Institutions, Regulators & Multilateral Bodies
audience: Board of Directors & Board Risk Committees
Chief AI Officer / CRO / CISO / CCO / GC
Systemic risk regulators (central banks, FSB, ESRB)
Treaty bodies and multilateral institutions (OECD, UN, IMF, BIS, G7/G20)
meta.scope: End-to-end civilizational governance stack treating AI as regulated critical infrastructure with memory, meaning, action, and legitimacy as first-class properties.

executiveSummary:
# Civilizational AI Governance Stack — 2026-2050+

## Thesis

Advanced AI — and on the decadal horizon, AGI/ASI-class systems — must be governed as
**regulated critical infrastructure**, on a footing equivalent to the payments rails,
sovereign debt markets, and nuclear safeguards regimes. A credible governance stack
cannot be a single policy document or a compliance checklist; it must be a **living
governance metabolism** — self-correcting under partial compliance, treaty-aligned,
globally interoperable, and anchored in a terminal governance attractor that aligns
**memory, meaning, action, and legitimacy**.

## Structure

This corpus is organized as a ten-module stack spanning three horizons:

| Horizon | Years | Primary Actors | Artefacts |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Enterprise & Frontier** | 2026-2030 | Financial institutions, frontier AI developers, sectoral regulators | Enterprise & Frontier Governance Architecture, Regulator Submission Pack, Kill-Switch Validation Protocol, Systemic Risk Simulation Playbook |
| **Multilateral** | 2028-2040 | Treaty bodies, standards organizations, coalitions | Global Interoperability & Treaty Alignment Framework, Global Operating Model, Pilot Deployment Roadmap, Coalition Activation Playbook, Global Governance Continuity Codex |
| **Civilizational** | 2035-2050+ | Civilizational institutions, regenerating covenants | Civilizational AI Governance Constitution, Ratification Ceremony, Covenant Codex & Canon, Global Renewal Atlas, Institutional Adoption Playbook, Terminal Governance Attractor |

## Core claims

1. **Governance metabolism** — the stack must metabolize signals (incidents, research,
   norms, geopolitics) into regulatory and operational updates on fixed cadences.
2. **Decision-discipline under uncertainty** — probabilistic risk tiering, explicit
   confidence intervals on every critical decision, and pre-committed response rules.
3. **Self-correcting under partial compliance** — the stack must function when coverage
   is incomplete (jurisdictions unratified, entities non-compliant) and pull the system
   toward completeness without coercion breakdown.
4. **Terminal governance attractor** — the long-run equilibrium is a four-dimensional
   attractor: Memory (evidence, audit trail, canon), Meaning (values, purposes, rights),
   Action (controls, interventions, kill-switches), Legitimacy (consent, ratification,
   stewardship). Deviation on any dimension triggers metabolic correction.
5. **Interoperability over hegemony** — divergent jurisdictional regimes are reconciled
   via **equivalence certificates**, mutual-recognition arrangements, and a shared
   technical substrate (provenance, evidence, evaluation) rather than imposed uniformity.

## Headline instruments

- **Civilizational AI Governance Constitution** — 14 first principles binding all
  participating institutions; renewable every 7 years via Ratification Ceremony.
- **Covenant Codex & Canon** — living body of inscribed practice, evidence, and
  precedent; machine-verifiable and cryptographically anchored.
- **Global Renewal Atlas** — technical architecture + reference implementation +
  pilot scenarios + multi-year lifecycle; maintained as open infrastructure.
- **Kill-Switch Validation Protocol (KSVP)** — quarterly validation drill with
  regulator observer; MTTK ≤60s; cross-system cascade containment ≤15 min.
- **Systemic AI Risk Simulation Playbook (SARSP)** — annual coordinated
  simulation across sectors and jurisdictions, modelled on CCAR stress tests.
- **Eight core indices** — civilizational AI risk barometer (CAI-RB), systemic
  coupling index, model-concentration Herfindahl, assurance depth index,
  regulatory equivalence index, covenant health, renewal velocity, attractor
  deviation.

## What the reader gets

This dashboard and API make the entire corpus **machine-addressable**. Every principle,
protocol, index, horizon, attractor dimension, and pilot scenario is queryable as JSON;
every page of the dashboard is a live view over the underlying evidence model; every
artefact cross-references regulatory citations and downstream controls.

m1_foundations.title: Foundations & Governance Metabolism
m1_foundations.summary: Core principles, governance metabolism model, decision-discipline under uncertainty, regulatory-alignment backbone.

sections.title: 14 First Principles
sections.content: Drawn from the Civilizational AI Governance Constitution (Module 7). These principles bind every downstream artefact and are the invariants against which self-correction is measured.

principles.name: Human Primacy
principles.statement: AI systems serve human flourishing; autonomy is bounded by human oversight at every critical decision.

principles.name: Regulated Critical Infrastructure
principles.statement: Frontier and systemic AI are governed with the same rigor as payments rails, nuclear safeguards, and aviation safety.

principles.name: Proportionate Risk Tiering
principles.statement: Obligations scale with capability, autonomy, and blast radius.

principles.name: Memory Integrity
principles.statement: Every decision, evaluation, and intervention is preserved in a tamper-evident record with 25+ year retention for civilizational-class systems.

principles.name: Meaning Preservation
principles.statement: Values, rights, purposes, and cultural context must be legible to systems and reviewable by humans; no loss of meaning through intermediation.

principles.name: Bounded Action
principles.statement: Every AI action is scoped by capability manifest, budget, and kill-switch with maximum time-to-kill (MTTK) ≤60s.

principles.name: Continuous Legitimacy
principles.statement: Consent is not granted once — it is renewed through ratification, public transparency, and stewardship review.

principles.name: Interoperable Equivalence
principles.statement: Jurisdictional divergence is reconciled via mutual-recognition, not hegemony; equivalence certificates have legal force.

principles.name: Evidence Over Assertion
principles.statement: All claims about capability, safety, and conformity must be supported by machine-verifiable evidence in the Covenant Codex.

principles.name: Metabolic Cadence
principles.statement: Signals are metabolized into governance updates on fixed cadences: daily ops, weekly risk, monthly committee, quarterly regulator, annual constitution review.

principles.name: Self-Correction
principles.statement: Partial compliance triggers automatic remediation workflows; the system must pull itself toward completeness without coercion breakdown.

principles.name: Fair Externalities
principles.statement: Costs and benefits of AI deployment must not concentrate on those without voice in governance.

principles.name: Stewardship Succession
principles.statement: No institution is indispensable; every protocol has a named successor regime, tested in simulation.

principles.name: Renewable Covenant
principles.statement: The constitution is renewed every seven years via Ratification Ceremony; no permanent rules without periodic re-consent.

sections.title: Governance Metabolism Model
sections.content: A six-loop metabolism: sense → classify → decide → act → evidence → renew. Each loop has a target cadence, owner, and KPI.

loops.description: Telemetry from AI systems, incidents, red-team, research, geopolitics.
Tier assignment, threat modelling, regulator-exposure mapping.

loops.cadence: Daily ops / Weekly risk / Monthly council
loops.description: Human-in-the-loop approvals, exception acceptance, policy updates.
Policy review, constitutional renewal, covenant ceremony.

sections.title: Decision-Discipline Under Uncertainty
sections.content: Seven rules for decisions where evidence is incomplete, contested, or adversarial.
rules: Every Critical/High decision records a probability distribution, not a point estimate.
Pre-committed response rules (if-then) are logged before the event and cannot be weakened under pressure without board override.
Decisions with >20% tail-risk of irreversible harm require unanimous AISRB sign-off + regulator pre-notification.
Dissent is recorded and preserved; unanimity without dissent for three consecutive cycles triggers review for capture.
Time-boxed decisions have an explicit default (fail-safe) that activates if consensus is not reached.
Precedent is consulted but not binding; every decision includes a 'why this differs' note when departing from past practice.
Reversibility is preferred over optimality; reversible actions are always admissible, irreversible ones require escalation.

sections.title: Regulatory Alignment Backbone
sections.content: Single control backbone mapping the entire stack to major regulatory frameworks, with equivalence indicators.

frameworks.alignment: Native — GOVERN/MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE is the operational shape of the metabolism model.
Certifiable — all M2/M3 controls map to Annex A; annual surveillance audit built in.
High-risk conformity packaged in Regulator Submission Pack (M3).
Art. 22 meaningful-information, Art. 35 DPIA, Art. 17 erasure propagation — all inline.
Incident reporting (24h/72h/30d), ICT third-party register, TLPT.
Model risk lifecycle mirrored in Enterprise/Frontier architecture; challenger models mandated for systemic use-cases.
Operational risk capital charges reflect AI concentration and autonomy level.
Systemic AI coupling index feeds into IMF/FSB systemic risk monitoring.
Declarations instantiated via equivalence certificates in M5.

m2_enterpriseFrontier.title: Enterprise & Frontier AGI/ASI Governance Architecture (2026-2030)
m2_enterpriseFrontier.summary: The operational architecture for financial institutions and frontier developers across the first horizon.

sections.title: Architectural Stack
sections.content: Six-layer enterprise stack (Infra/Data/Model/App/Agent/Governance) is embedded; additionally, a Frontier tier adds capability evaluations, pre-deployment red-team, and compute-threshold gating.

tiers.scope: AI in critical/important functions under DORA or SR 11-7
Foundation models ≥10^25 FLOPs or systemic-impact GPAI (EU AI Act Art. 55)
extras: Safety case document with formal argument

tiers.scope: Systems with broad cross-domain capability comparable to a trained expert across 70%+ cognitive tasks
Systems plausibly exceeding collective-human performance on open-ended tasks
extras: Deployment only within sandbox; outputs reviewed pre-release

sections.title: Frontier Capability Evaluations
sections.content: Standardized evaluation suite for AGI/ASI-candidate tiers, with public methodology and independent replication requirement.

evaluations.threshold: Behavior inconsistent across oversight/no-oversight contexts → block
Deployment rate exceeds sectoral rebalancing capacity → phased rollout mandated

sections.title: Frontier Safety Case Structure
sections.content: Each frontier deployment must produce a safety case — a structured, machine-verifiable argument that residual risk is tolerable.
structure: Claim: the deployment is safe for intended use in intended context
Argument graph: sub-claims with dependency structure
Evidence: evaluations, red-team, monitoring plan, external review
Assumptions log: every assumption with invalidation-trigger
Residual risk accepted: by whom, on what authority, for what period
Renewal date: ≤12 months; earlier on any invalidation-trigger

sections.title: Closing Charge
sections.content: For each frontier deployment cycle, the AI Safety Review Board issues a Closing Charge — a written determination that: (a) the safety case meets the standard of care for the tier; (b) the residual risk is within risk appetite; (c) monitoring and rollback plans are validated; and (d) the decision is open to regulator and public challenge for 30 days after issuance. Absent a Closing Charge, no frontier deployment proceeds.

template.signing: Ed25519 quorum (3-of-5 AISRB members + CAIO); published to Covenant Codex

m3_regulatorSubmission.title: Regulator Submission Pack & Compliance Instruments
m3_regulatorSubmission.summary: Standardized submission pack for high-risk / frontier systems with artefact manifest, hashes, and navigable evidence.

sections.title: Submission Pack Manifest
sections.content: Standardized JSON manifest accompanies every regulator submission; hashes bind to Covenant Codex.

manifest.maps: EU AI Act Annex IV §2, GDPR Art. 30
EU AI Act Annex IV §4, ISO 42001 clause 6
EU AI Act Art. 73, NIS2 Art. 23, DORA Art. 17

sections.title: Submission Workflow
sections.content: End-to-end workflow from intake to closure, with SLAs and escalation triggers.
steps: T-60d: draft safety case + evaluation results to regulator
T-30d: regulator questions; response within 10 business days
T-14d: final submission with Closing Charge

sections.title: Compliance Instruments
sections.content: Menu of standard instruments regulators and supervised entities can invoke.

instruments.name: No-Action Letter
instruments.purpose: Regulator forbearance during pilot or migration

instruments.name: Sandbox Authorization
instruments.purpose: Time-boxed trial with bounded scope and observers

instruments.name: Exit Plan Activation
instruments.purpose: Ordered unwind of a critical third-party AI

m4_killSwitchSimulation.title: Kill-Switch Validation & Systemic AI Risk Simulation
m4_killSwitchSimulation.summary: Quarterly KSVP drills and annual SARSP coordinated simulations.

sections.title: Kill-Switch Validation Protocol (KSVP)
sections.content: Quarterly validated drill; regulator observer present for Tier ≥ Enterprise-Systemic; results published in Covenant Codex.
protocol: Preparation (T-14d): scope, systems in drill, safety review, communications plan
Notification (T-7d): regulator + board informed; public abstract for transparency
Execution (T-0): live kill-switch invocation; measure MTTK + blast-radius containment
Containment validation: state of all dependent systems at T+5min, T+15min, T+1h, T+24h
Post-mortem (T+3d): blameless RCA; publish in Covenant Codex
Remediation (T+30d): all items closed or escalated to AISRB

sections.title: Systemic AI Risk Simulation Playbook (SARSP)
sections.content: Annual coordinated simulation across sectors and jurisdictions, modelled on CCAR-style stress tests.
components: Scenario library (e.g., prompt-injection at scale on LLM-mediated financial advice; mass hallucination in medical triage; weights-poisoning of widely-used foundation model; GPAI critical vulnerability on a weekend; cross-border infra AI failure)
Participant tiers: frontier developers + major deployers + regulators + CERTs + treaty observers
Run configurations: tabletop, live-fire (with production-shadow systems), adversarial red team
Metrics: systemic loss function, fair-sharing of response burden, containment velocity
Publication: top-line results public within 60 days; classified full results to participants under NDA

scenarios.name: LLM Financial Advice Storm
scenarios.vector: Prompt-injection in retrieved financial policy → inconsistent advice at scale

scenarios.name: Weekend GPAI CVE
scenarios.vector: Zero-day in foundation model; patch requires weights update

scenarios.name: Grid Copilot Cascade
scenarios.vector: Faulty recommendation agent propagates across operators

scenarios.name: Cross-Border Infra AI Failure
scenarios.vector: Coordinated failure in AI managing cross-border payments / settlement

scenarios.name: Frontier Model Self-Exfiltration
scenarios.vector: Attempt at unauthorized egress from sandbox

sections.title: Cross-Switch Coordination
sections.content: Kill-switches across institutions cannot be independent — cascading failures require coordinated switching.
mechanisms: Shared Kill-Switch Registry (KSR) at treaty-body level
Dry-run obligations for cross-institution dependencies annually
Public-interest override: treaty body can request coordinated switch for systemic events

m5_interopTreatyOpModel.title: Global Interoperability, Treaty Alignment & Operating Model
m5_interopTreatyOpModel.summary: How divergent jurisdictions reconcile, and who operates the global stack.

sections.title: Interoperability Framework
sections.content: Equivalence certificates, shared technical substrate, and mutual-recognition arrangements replace imposed uniformity.

layers.content: OECD + UNESCO + Hiroshima + Bletchley + Seoul principles — non-negotiable baseline
Bilateral / plurilateral mutual-recognition agreements; equivalence certificates
Common evidence format, model cards, evaluation suites, provenance (C2PA), SBOM for models
Shared incident taxonomy + KSR + SARSP scenarios + regulatory data exchange

equivalenceCertificate.description: Machine-verifiable record that Regime A satisfies the requirements of Regime B for a given class of systems.

sections.title: Global AI Governance Operating Model
sections.content: Four-ring model: institutional → sectoral → national → multilateral, with defined signal-flow between rings.
sections.signalFlow: R1→R2→R3→R4 for aggregation; R4→R3→R2→R1 for policy diffusion; each hop has a maximum latency and quality gate.

sections.title: Coalition Activation Playbook
sections.content: For crises or common-mode risks, coalitions of the willing activate coordinated response without waiting for full treaty consensus.
stages: Trigger: incident, vulnerability, or frontier capability crossing a threshold
Situational report: shared within 96h under common NDA
Coordinated action: joint statement + technical measures + timeline for wider ratification
Institutionalization: coalition measures folded into treaty update within 18 months

m6_pilotRoadmapCoalition.title: Global Pilot Deployment Roadmap & Coalition Activation
m6_pilotRoadmapCoalition.summary: Phased deployment from pilot to global with seven reference scenarios.

sections.title: Pilot Phases
sections.content: Five phases across 2026-2032 with clear exit criteria.

phases.scope: All systemic institutions in a sector

sections.title: Reference Pilot Scenarios
sections.content: Seven pilot scenarios spanning financial, health, energy, public, defense-adjacent, frontier, and cross-border.

pilots.name: G-SIFI Systemic AI Pilot
pilots.focus: Credit decisioning + KYC autonomous triage; model-risk + DORA + EU AI Act coverage

pilots.name: Pharmacovigilance Consortium
pilots.focus: Shared safety-signal evaluation; FDA PCCP harmonized with EMA

pilots.name: Grid Copilot Interop
pilots.focus: Cross-border control-room copilot with joint kill-switch

pilots.name: Public-Sector AI Transparency
pilots.focus: Algorithmic transparency records with shared schema

sections.title: Coalition Activation Workflow
sections.content: Codified in Coalition Activation Playbook (CAP); same as M5-S3 but with specific timelines and pre-commitments.

m7_continuityConstitution.title: Governance Continuity Codex & Civilizational AI Governance Constitution

sections.title: Global Governance Continuity Codex (GGCC)
sections.content: A procedural book-of-record ensuring governance continues through crises, leadership changes, and institutional failures.
contents: Line-of-succession for every critical role (CAIO → deputy → external custodian)
Crisis decision authority (who can act, for how long, with what quorum)
Data-survival protocols (evidence vault redundancy, cryptographic anchoring)
Ex-post review: every emergency action reviewed within 90 days

sections.title: Civilizational AI Governance Constitution
sections.content: Binding foundational document for all participating institutions; 14 articles mirroring the 14 principles (M1-S1).

articles.title: Human Primacy
articles.essence: All AI systems are instruments serving human flourishing under human oversight.

articles.title: Regulated Critical Infrastructure
articles.essence: Frontier AI is governed with rigor equal to payments rails and nuclear safeguards.

articles.title: Proportionate Risk Tiering
articles.essence: Obligations scale with capability, autonomy, and blast radius.

articles.title: Memory
articles.essence: Tamper-evident record of decisions and evidence is preserved across generations.

articles.title: Meaning
articles.essence: Values and purposes are legible and reviewable; meaning cannot be lost in intermediation.

articles.title: Action
articles.essence: Every action is bounded by manifest and kill-switch.

articles.title: Legitimacy
articles.essence: Consent is renewed through ratification and stewardship.

articles.title: Evidence
articles.essence: All claims supported by verifiable evidence.

articles.title: Fair Externalities
articles.essence: Burdens and benefits must not concentrate on the voiceless.

articles.title: Stewardship Succession
articles.essence: No institution is indispensable; succession is tested.

articles.title: Renewable Covenant
articles.essence: The constitution is renewed every seven years.

sections.amendment: Amendments require 2/3 super-majority of ratifying parties at a Ratification Ceremony.
sections.sunset: Automatic renewal required every 7 years; absent renewal, the constitution lapses and fallback regime activates.

m8_ceremonyCodexCanon.title: Ratification Ceremony, Covenant Codex & Performance Protocol
m8_ceremonyCodexCanon.summary: How the constitution is instantiated, evidenced, and renewed.

sections.title: Ratification Ceremony Playbook
sections.content: Ceremonial + legal + technical instantiation of constitutional renewal.
stages: Convening (T-12m): treaty body announces, working groups formed
Deliberation (T-9m to T-3m): public consultation, drafting updates
Civic inscription (T-3m): public-commentary period; dissents recorded
Ratification (T-0): signing ceremony, cryptographic co-signature, broadcast
Inscription (T+30d): constitution + dissents + equivalence certificates entered into Covenant Codex
Canon update (T+90d): Covenant Codex Canon republished with new text
Operational rollout (T+365d): all downstream controls updated
sections.ceremony: Combination of: (a) cryptographic group-signing by accredited parties; (b) public transparency broadcast; (c) symbolic civic act recognized by participating legal systems.

sections.title: Civilizational Covenant Codex
sections.content: Canonical, append-only, cryptographically anchored body of inscribed practice, evidence, and precedent.
properties: Append-only (no deletions); corrections are new entries
Public portal with search, navigation, export

sections.title: Codex Canon
sections.content: Curated, authoritative subset of the Covenant Codex representing binding precedent.
layers: Canon L2 — Treaty-level protocols (binding on ratifying parties)
Canon L3 — Sectoral standards (binding on sector)
Canon L4 — Institutional practice (binding on institution)

sections.title: Inscription and Performance Protocol
sections.content: How practice becomes evidence and evidence becomes canon.
flow: Challenge: 30-day open challenge window for any promotion

m9_renewalAtlasAdoption.title: Global Renewal Atlas & Institutional Adoption Playbook

sections.title: Renewal Atlas — Technical Architecture
sections.content: Open-source, public-interest technical stack implementing the governance metabolism.

sections.title: Reference Implementation
sections.content: Reference open-source implementation meeting all functional & non-functional requirements.
nfrs: Latency: <200ms p99 for read, <500ms for write
Portability: Kubernetes + standard object storage; no vendor lock-in
Transparency: 100% of code and policies public; audited
Replicability: ≥3 independent regional stewards per region

sections.title: Multi-Year Lifecycle
sections.content: Lifecycle management of the Renewal Atlas across constitutional cycles.
phases: Y1-2: Convergence with major regional regimes
Y3-4: Sectoral onboarding; equivalence certificate network established
Y8+: New cycle; legacy gradually sunsetted

sections.title: Institutional Adoption Playbook
sections.content: How a financial institution, regulator, or multilateral body onboards.
playbook: Gap closure plan with board approval
Inscription of first evidence bundle in Covenant Codex

m10_attractorStewardship.title: Terminal Governance Attractor, Stewardship Roadmap & Terminal Closure
m10_attractorStewardship.summary: The long-run equilibrium and closure semantics.

sections.title: Terminal Governance Attractor
sections.content: Four-dimensional attractor to which a self-correcting governance system converges. Deviation on any dimension triggers metabolic correction; simultaneous deviation on three or more triggers treaty-level intervention.

dimensions.failureMode: Evidence loss, record rot, unverifiable claims

dimensions.invariant: Values + rights + purposes legible end-to-end; no semantic drift >0.05/year
dimensions.failureMode: Value capture, purpose creep, translation loss

dimensions.invariant: Every AI action scoped + kill-switchable; MTTK ≤60s
dimensions.failureMode: Unbounded autonomy, orphaned agents, sovereign tools

dimensions.invariant: Consent renewed every 7y; dissent preserved; stewardship tested

sections.attractorDeviation: Composite distance metric d(A) = √(Σ w_i · (dim_i - invariant_i)²); d(A) < 0.15 is the zone of health.

sections.title: Stewardship Roadmap
sections.content: Who holds the stack, with what authority, for how long, and how they are replaced.
steward: Primary steward: accredited treaty body with international legal personality
Regional stewards: one per continent, rotating 5-year terms
Sectoral stewards: per critical sector, rotating 3-year terms
Ultimate authority: ratifying parties via Ratification Ceremony
Default steward: activated on primary failure; ex-ante named and rehearsed
succession: Every steward has a named successor tested annually
Stewardship is always bounded in term; no permanent roles
Conflicts of interest disclosed and managed
Removal for cause: 2/3 super-majority of ratifying parties

sections.title: Self-Correcting Governance Under Partial Compliance
sections.content: Mechanisms that pull toward completeness when parties are non-compliant or absent.
mechanisms: Partial-coverage equivalence: certificates valid where coverage exists, limited elsewhere
Graduated obligations: new entrants onboard in tiers with lighter initial obligations
Positive-incentive alignment: insurance discounts, capital relief, market access conditional on participation
Reputation markets: public compliance scores create pressure without coercion
Escape-valve: non-compliant parties may opt into a sandbox regime with time-boxed exemptions
Universal obligations: a minimal core (memory + kill-switch + incident reporting) applies regardless of ratification

sections.title: Terminal Closure & Dissolution Protocol
sections.content: If the stack must be dissolved (e.g., superseded by successor regime, existential rethink after ASI emergence, civilizational restructuring), closure is orderly and preserves the record.
protocol: Trigger: 2/3 super-majority resolution at Ratification Ceremony
Record handover: Covenant Codex sealed, cryptographically anchored, archived to regional stewards + UNESCO World Digital Library equivalent
Obligation transition: successor regime defined or fallback to universal minimum
Ceremony of closure: public event; honors dissent; recognizes stewards
Post-closure: Codex remains readable for 100+ years; no rewriting

sections.title: Closing Charge — Civilizational
sections.content: The civilizational Closing Charge is issued once per seven-year cycle by the treaty body: a written determination that the stack has preserved memory, meaning, action, and legitimacy within tolerances; that stewardship succession is tested; and that the next cycle begins with the record intact. Absent a civilizational Closing Charge, the terminal closure protocol activates.

indices.name: Civilizational AI Risk Barometer (CAI-RB)
indices.description: Composite index reflecting aggregate risk posture across participating institutions; published monthly by treaty body.
indices.trigger: >70 triggers FSB-level review; >85 triggers coalition activation

indices.name: Systemic AI Coupling Index
indices.description: Measures cross-dependency density of AI systems in critical functions.

indices.name: Model Concentration Herfindahl
indices.description: Market-share concentration across foundation model vendors in systemic functions.
indices.trigger: >40% single-vendor share in a sector triggers anti-concentration review

indices.name: Assurance Depth Index
indices.description: How deeply each deployment is assured (red-team frequency, eval breadth, evidence freshness).

indices.name: Regulatory Equivalence Index
indices.description: Degree of mutual recognition between jurisdictions for AI governance.

indices.name: Covenant Health
indices.description: Health of the Covenant Codex (integrity, freshness, accessibility, participation).

indices.name: Renewal Velocity
indices.description: How quickly the system metabolizes signal into governance update.

indices.name: Attractor Deviation (d_A)
indices.description: Composite distance from the terminal attractor across the four dimensions.

architecture.description: Five-plane civilizational architecture sitting on top of the WP-030 enterprise six-layer stack. The enterprise stack serves one institution; this architecture federates across institutions, sectors, jurisdictions, and eventually civilizational scope.
architecture.relationship: The five civilizational planes consume and amplify signals from the six enterprise layers (WP-030); each enterprise control instance produces evidence that becomes a leaf in the Evidence Plane's Merkle-DAG.

caseStudies.name: G-SIFI Credit-Decisioning Systemic Pilot (2027-2029)
caseStudies.participants: 4 G-SIFIs across UK/US/EU/SG + 3 sectoral regulators + BIS observer
caseStudies.scope: Credit decisioning + KYC autonomous triage under mutual recognition
caseStudies.lesson: Mutual recognition is feasible when technical substrate is shared; lesson exported to PI-7.

caseStudies.name: Frontier Developer Compact (2028)
caseStudies.lesson: Voluntary regime stabilized the period between 2027 and first treaty ratification.

caseStudies.name: Grid Copilot Interop (2027)
caseStudies.scope: Cross-border control-room copilot with joint kill-switch
caseStudies.lesson: Coordinated KSR works; blueprint for payments AI pilot.

caseStudies.name: Pharmacovigilance Consortium (2028-2030)
caseStudies.participants: EU EMA + US FDA + JP PMDA + 11 pharma
caseStudies.lesson: Sectoral harmonization precedes constitutional ratification; case for M6 sectoral phase.

caseStudies.name: First Civilizational Ratification Ceremony (2032 projected)
caseStudies.participants: UN-class membership + treaty body + accredited institutions
caseStudies.scope: Inaugural signing of Civilizational AI Governance Constitution
caseStudies.lesson: Ceremony is ritual + cryptography + legal act; all three required for legitimacy.

codeExamples.attractorDeviation:
# Attractor Deviation — composite distance from the terminal attractor.
import math

def attractor_deviation(memory: float, meaning: float, action: float, legitimacy: float,
                         weights=(0.30, 0.25, 0.25, 0.20),
                         invariants=(1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0)) -> dict:
    """Return d_A and zone. Each dim in [0, 1], where 1 = ideal alignment."""
    dims = (memory, meaning, action, legitimacy)
    w_sum = sum(w * (i - d) ** 2 for w, d, i in zip(weights, dims, invariants))
    d = math.sqrt(w_sum)
    if   d < 0.15: zone = 'Healthy'
    elif d < 0.30: zone = 'Drifting'
    elif d < 0.50: zone = 'Corrective'
    else:          zone = 'Crisis — coalition activation'
    return {'d_A': d, 'zone': zone, 'dims': {'memory': memory, 'meaning': meaning,
                                              'action': action, 'legitimacy': legitimacy}}
codeExamples.sarspYaml:
# SARSP scenario definition (excerpt)
scenario:
  id: SC-1
  name: LLM Financial Advice Storm
  sector: FSI
  vector: prompt-injection in retrieved policy
  participants:
    frontierLabs: 3
    deployers: 12
    regulators: [PRA, Fed, BaFin, MAS]
    observers: [BIS, FSB]
  phases:
    - name: preparation
      duration_days: 14
      deliverables: [threat_model, communication_plan, dry_run_results]
    - name: live_fire
      duration_hours: 8
      entry_criteria: [dry_run_pass, regulator_ack]
      metrics:
        - name: systemic_loss_function
          unit: USDm
          threshold_red: 500
        - name: containment_velocity
          unit: minutes
          target: <=15
    - name: post_mortem
      duration_days: 3
      deliverables: [blameless_rca, canon_inscription]
  success_criteria:
    - systemic_loss_function < 500
    - containment_velocity <= 15
    - 0 unrecovered subsystems at T+24h
